metadata.title: Base-Rate Bayes Calculator: Why High Sensitivity Isn't Enough

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Konnichiwa, Liam — in for Bear. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Build it with a CLI, then take it apart?
props.segment: Base-Rate Bayes Calculator: Why High Sensitivity

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: A hospital deploys a sepsis detection model with ninety percent sensitivity and eighty-two percent specificity. The ICU team is told: when it fires, the patient probably has sepsis. But in a population where sepsis occurs in two percent of admissions, the positive predictive value — the chance the alert is real — is under ten percent. Nine out of ten alerts are false. The model is not broken. The base rate is. This video shows why sensitivity and specificity are not enough, and how Bayes' theorem tells you the number that actually matters.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'sensitivity=90%, PPV=9%' — the base rate gap; PROBLEM beat, no terminal

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to write a Bayes PPV function, then compute a five-by-five grid over prevalence and sensitivity at a fixed specificity of point-eight-two — the sepsis model's published value — and mark every cell where PPV drops below ten percent.

props.command:
claude "Write bayes_ppv(prevalence, sensitivity, specificity)
using Bayes' theorem. Compute 5x5 grid:
  prevalence=[0.005,0.01,0.02,0.05,0.10]
  sensitivity=[0.70,0.80,0.85,0.90,0.99]
  specificity=0.82
Print as pandas DataFrame.
Mark cells where PPV < 0.10."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — read the code before running
beats.narration_text: Read the key formula before running. PPV equals sensitivity times prevalence, divided by sensitivity times prevalence plus one-minus-specificity times one-minus-prevalence. The denominator is where the base rate lives. When prevalence is point-zero-zero-five — half a percent — that denominator is dominated by the false positive rate, and sensitivity can't save you.

props.code:
# bayes_ppv.py  —  Claude Code output

def bayes_ppv(prevalence, sensitivity, specificity):
    """Positive Predictive Value via Bayes' theorem."""
    tp = sensitivity * prevalence
    fp = (1 - specificity) * (1 - prevalence)
    ppv = tp / (tp + fp)
    return ppv

# At prevalence=0.02, sensitivity=0.90, specificity=0.82:
# ppv = (0.90 * 0.02) / (0.90*0.02 + 0.18*0.98)
#     = 0.018 / (0.018 + 0.1764)
#     = 0.018 / 0.1944  ≈ 0.093  <- under 10%

beats.narration_text: Run it. The heatmap shows twenty-five cells: prevalence on rows, sensitivity on columns. The top two rows — prevalence under one percent — are entirely crimson. Every cell is below ten percent PPV, regardless of how high sensitivity goes. The gold band in rows three and four is the transition zone. Only when prevalence reaches ten percent do you start seeing green — and even then, PPV tops out at thirty-five percent with ninety-nine percent sensitivity.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_BayesHeatmap: 5x5 PPV grid colored crimson/gold/green by threshold; sepsis operating point highlighted; 'PPV < 10% zone' annotation on top 2 rows

beats.narration_text: The grid is the audit. Now iterate: ask Claude to add a specificity sweep — hold sensitivity at ninety percent, vary specificity from point-seven to point-nine-nine, and show at what specificity the PPV at two-percent prevalence finally crosses the ten-percent threshold.

props.command:
claude "Update bayes_ppv.py:
-> fix sensitivity=0.90, prevalence=0.02
-> sweep specificity from 0.70 to 0.99
-> find the specificity where PPV crosses 0.10
-> print threshold and print a small table"

beats.narration_text: At two-percent prevalence with ninety-percent sensitivity, you need a specificity of ninety-four percent before PPV clears ten percent. The sepsis model published at eighty-two percent never makes it. The lesson: sensitivity and specificity are properties of the test. PPV is a property of the deployment context. They are not interchangeable, and the base rate is not optional information.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'test properties vs deployment context'; specificity threshold result

beats.narration_text: Your move: find the base rate of the condition your model detects in the real deployment population — not the training set. Plug it into the Bayes PPV formula with the published sensitivity and specificity. If PPV is under twenty percent, your alert system needs a prevalence-enriched pre-filter before it goes clinical.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) find real deployment base rate, 2) compute PPV, 3) add pre-filter if PPV < 20%

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Your move: find the base rate of the condition your model detects in the real deployment population — not the training set. Plug it into the Bayes PPV formula with the published sensitivity and specificity. If PPV is under twenty percent, your alert system needs a prevalence-enriched pre-filter before it goes clinical.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Base-Rate Bayes Calculator: Why High Sensitivity Isn't Enough] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
